meta.notes: Physics (CPBS) mechanics items: kinematics, Newton's laws/forces, and gravitation. Stems and four options scraped verbatim from OpenStax College Physics for AP Courses 2e 'Test Prep for AP Courses' single-best-answer multiple-choice items. OpenStax publishes the answer key to instructors only, so each correctAnswer was independently verified from the same CC BY-NC-SA source text or by direct calculation (answerProvenance = verified-from-source-text). Items requiring an un-capturable figure/graph, 5-option items, 'select two' items, and free-response items were skipped. Ch. 5 (Friction/Drag/Elasticity) test-prep page timed out on fetch and was skipped. One item (cpbs-xp034) is based on a figure whose forces are fully given in the question text.

questions.stem: Which of the following statements comparing position, distance traveled, and displacement is correct?

choices.label: A
choices.text: An object may record a distance traveled of zero while recording a non-zero displacement.

choices.label: B
choices.text: An object may record a non-zero distance traveled while recording a displacement of zero.

choices.label: C
choices.text: An object may record a non-zero distance traveled while maintaining a position of zero.

choices.label: D
choices.text: An object may record a non-zero displacement while maintaining a position of zero.

questions.explanation: Distance is the total path length and is never negative; displacement is the net change in position. An object that travels out and returns to its start (a round trip) covers a non-zero distance but has zero displacement. The reverse (distance zero, displacement non-zero) is impossible.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax College Physics for AP® Courses 2e, Ch. 2 (Kinematics) — Test Prep for AP® Courses
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax; live page also posts a no-LLM-training notice — SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Stem + 4 options scraped verbatim. OpenStax withholds the public answer key; answer independently verified from the definitions of distance and displacement.

hints.prompt: Which statement correctly distinguishes distance traveled from displacement?

choices.label: A
choices.text: distance is the total path length (never negative); displacement is the net change in position

choices.label: B
choices.text: distance and displacement are always equal

choices.label: C
choices.text: displacement is always larger than distance

choices.label: D
choices.text: distance can be negative

hints.rationale: Distance is the total path length (≥0); displacement is the net change in position.

hints.prompt: Can an object ever record zero distance traveled but a NONzero displacement?

choices.label: A
choices.text: no—any change in position requires some path length, so zero distance forces zero displacement

choices.label: B
choices.text: yes, if it moves fast enough

choices.label: C
choices.text: yes, if it returns to its start

choices.label: D
choices.text: yes, but only in two dimensions

hints.rationale: Displacement can never exceed the distance traveled, so zero distance means zero displacement.

hints.prompt: Displacement can never be larger than the distance traveled, and an object keeping a fixed position isn't moving at all. Which pair of options can be eliminated as self-contradictory?

choices.label: A
choices.text: 'distance of zero while recording a non-zero displacement' and 'non-zero displacement while maintaining a position of zero'

choices.label: B
choices.text: 'non-zero distance while recording a displacement of zero' and 'non-zero distance while maintaining a position of zero'

choices.label: C
choices.text: 'distance of zero while recording a non-zero displacement' and 'non-zero distance while recording a displacement of zero'

choices.label: D
choices.text: 'non-zero displacement while maintaining a position of zero' and 'non-zero distance while recording a displacement of zero'

hints.rationale: Zero distance forces zero displacement, and a truly fixed position means no displacement, so both of those are impossible.

questions.stem: A student is trying to determine the acceleration of a feather as she drops it to the ground. If the student is looking to achieve a positive velocity and positive acceleration, what is the most sensible way to set up her coordinate system?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Her hand should be a coordinate of zero and the upward direction should be considered positive.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Her hand should be a coordinate of zero and the downward direction should be considered positive.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The floor should be a coordinate of zero and the upward direction should be considered positive.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The floor should be a coordinate of zero and the downward direction should be considered positive.

questions.explanation: The feather moves and accelerates downward. To make both the velocity and the acceleration come out positive, the downward direction must be defined as positive. Placing the origin at the release point (her hand) also makes the position positive as the feather falls.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax College Physics for AP® Courses 2e, Ch. 2 (Kinematics) — Test Prep for AP® Courses
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax; live page also posts a no-LLM-training notice — SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Stem + 4 options scraped verbatim. OpenStax withholds the public answer key; answer independently verified (sign of velocity/acceleration follows the chosen positive direction).

hints.prompt: The sign (positive or negative) of an object's velocity and acceleration depends on:

choices.label: A
choices.text: the direction chosen as positive for the coordinate system

choices.label: B
choices.text: the object's mass

choices.label: C
choices.text: nothing—they are always positive

choices.label: D
choices.text: the time of day

hints.rationale: Signs are set by which direction you define as positive.

hints.prompt: The feather moves and accelerates DOWNWARD. To make both its velocity and acceleration come out positive, which direction must be defined as positive?

choices.label: A
choices.text: downward

choices.label: B
choices.text: upward

choices.label: C
choices.text: horizontal

choices.label: D
choices.text: either direction works equally

hints.rationale: Because the motion and acceleration are downward, downward must be the positive direction.

hints.prompt: You have determined the positive direction must be downward. Which pair of options can be eliminated because they take UPWARD as positive?

choices.label: A
choices.text: 'hand = 0, upward positive' and 'floor = 0, upward positive'

choices.label: B
choices.text: 'hand = 0, downward positive' and 'floor = 0, downward positive'

choices.label: C
choices.text: 'hand = 0, upward positive' and 'hand = 0, downward positive'

choices.label: D
choices.text: 'floor = 0, upward positive' and 'floor = 0, downward positive'

hints.rationale: Upward-positive would make the downward velocity and acceleration negative, so both upward-positive options are eliminated.

questions.stem: A ball is thrown at an angle of 45 degrees above the horizontal. Which of the following best describes the acceleration of the ball from the instant after it leaves the thrower's hand until the time it hits the ground?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Always in the same direction as the motion, initially positive and gradually dropping to zero by the time it hits the ground

choices.label: B
choices.text: Initially positive in the upward direction, then zero at maximum height, then negative from there until it hits the ground

choices.label: C
choices.text: Always in the opposite direction as the motion, initially positive and gradually dropping to zero by the time it hits the ground

choices.label: D
choices.text: Always in the downward direction with the same constant value

questions.explanation: Once the ball is in free flight (neglecting air resistance), the only force on it is gravity, so its acceleration is constant: g directed downward at every instant, including at the top of the trajectory where the velocity is momentarily horizontal.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax College Physics for AP® Courses 2e, Ch. 3 (Two-Dimensional Kinematics) — Test Prep for AP® Courses
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax; live page also posts a no-LLM-training notice — SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Stem + 4 options scraped verbatim. OpenStax withholds the public answer key; answer independently verified (projectile acceleration is constant g downward).

hints.prompt: For a projectile in free flight (ignoring air resistance), the only force is gravity, so its acceleration is:

choices.label: A
choices.text: constant and directed downward at every instant

choices.label: B
choices.text: zero at the top of the path

choices.label: C
choices.text: always in the direction of motion

choices.label: D
choices.text: always opposite to the motion

hints.rationale: Gravity is the only force in free flight, giving a constant downward acceleration g.

hints.prompt: At the very top of the 45° trajectory, the ball's velocity is momentarily horizontal. What is its acceleration there?

choices.label: A
choices.text: g, directed downward (the same as everywhere else)

choices.label: B
choices.text: zero

choices.label: C
choices.text: horizontal, in the direction of motion

choices.label: D
choices.text: upward

hints.rationale: Even at the top gravity still acts, so the acceleration is g downward.

hints.prompt: Gravity does not depend on the direction of the ball's motion and never drops to zero during flight. Which pair of options can be eliminated?

choices.label: A
choices.text: 'always in the same direction as the motion, dropping to zero at the ground' and 'always opposite to the motion, dropping to zero at the ground'

choices.label: B
choices.text: 'initially up, zero at max height, then negative' and 'always downward with the same constant value'

choices.label: C
choices.text: 'always in the same direction as the motion...' and 'always downward with the same constant value'

choices.label: D
choices.text: 'always opposite to the motion...' and 'initially up, zero at max height, then negative'

hints.rationale: Acceleration is set by gravity, not by the motion's direction, and never falls to zero in flight, so both 'direction-of-motion' options are eliminated.

questions.stem: A student launches a ball with an initial horizontal velocity at an elevation 2 meters above ground. The ball follows a parabolic trajectory until it hits the ground. Which of the following accurately describes the graph of the ball's vertical acceleration versus time (taking the downward direction to be negative)?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A negative value that does not change with time

choices.label: B
choices.text: A gradually increasing negative value (straight line)

choices.label: C
choices.text: An increasing rate of negative values over time (parabolic curve)

choices.label: D
choices.text: Zero at all times since the initial motion is horizontal

questions.explanation: During projectile motion the vertical acceleration is constant at -g (about -9.8 m/s² with downward negative). It does not change with time, so its graph is a horizontal line at a constant negative value. The initial horizontal launch does not change the vertical acceleration.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax College Physics for AP® Courses 2e, Ch. 3 (Two-Dimensional Kinematics) — Test Prep for AP® Courses
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax; live page also posts a no-LLM-training notice — SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Stem + 4 options scraped verbatim. OpenStax withholds the public answer key; answer independently verified (vertical acceleration is the constant -g).

hints.prompt: During projectile motion, the VERTICAL acceleration is:

choices.label: A
choices.text: constant in time (equal to g)

choices.label: B
choices.text: steadily increasing in time

choices.label: C
choices.text: zero

choices.label: D
choices.text: changing sign partway through

hints.rationale: Projectile vertical acceleration is the constant g.

hints.prompt: The ball is launched HORIZONTALLY. How does the initial horizontal velocity affect the ball's vertical acceleration?

choices.label: A
choices.text: not at all—the vertical acceleration is still just g

choices.label: B
choices.text: it makes the vertical acceleration grow over time

choices.label: C
choices.text: it makes the vertical acceleration zero

choices.label: D
choices.text: it makes the vertical acceleration curve like a parabola

hints.rationale: Horizontal and vertical motions are independent, so the horizontal launch does not change the vertical acceleration g.

hints.prompt: There is a definite nonzero downward acceleration, and gravity's value is constant (not curved). Which pair of options can be eliminated?

choices.label: A
choices.text: 'an increasing rate of negative values (parabolic curve)' and 'zero at all times since the motion is horizontal'

choices.label: B
choices.text: 'a negative value that does not change with time' and 'a gradually increasing negative value (straight line)'

choices.label: C
choices.text: 'a negative value that does not change with time' and 'zero at all times...'

choices.label: D
choices.text: 'a gradually increasing negative value (straight line)' and 'an increasing rate of negative values (parabolic curve)'

hints.rationale: Gravity gives a constant, nonzero downward acceleration, so the 'parabolic' and 'zero' graphs are eliminated.

questions.stem: A ball is launched vertically upward. Which of the following correctly describes the graph of the ball's vertical velocity versus time, from just after launch until it returns to its starting height (taking up as positive)?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Always positive, steadily decreasing

choices.label: B
choices.text: Always positive, constant

choices.label: C
choices.text: Initially positive, steadily decreasing, becoming negative at the end

choices.label: D
choices.text: Initially zero, steadily getting more and more negative

questions.explanation: With constant downward acceleration (-g), the velocity decreases linearly. It starts positive (moving up), reaches zero at the highest point, then becomes negative as the ball falls back down. The graph is a straight line of constant negative slope crossing from positive to negative.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax College Physics for AP® Courses 2e, Ch. 3 (Two-Dimensional Kinematics) — Test Prep for AP® Courses
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax; live page also posts a no-LLM-training notice — SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Stem + 4 options scraped verbatim; the source provided a position-vs-time data table, summarized here as 'launched vertically upward.' OpenStax withholds the public answer key; answer independently verified.

hints.prompt: For an object under constant downward acceleration (−g), the velocity-vs-time graph is:

choices.label: A
choices.text: a straight line with constant negative slope

choices.label: B
choices.text: a horizontal line

choices.label: C
choices.text: a parabola

choices.label: D
choices.text: a line whose slope keeps increasing

hints.rationale: Constant acceleration means velocity changes linearly, a straight line of constant slope.

hints.prompt: At the highest point of the upward-launched ball's path, its instantaneous velocity is:

choices.label: A
choices.text: zero

choices.label: B
choices.text: maximum

choices.label: C
choices.text: equal to its launch velocity

choices.label: D
choices.text: still pointing upward

hints.rationale: At the top the ball momentarily stops, so its velocity is zero before it reverses.

hints.prompt: The ball is launched upward, so it starts with a positive velocity that must change under gravity. Which pair of options can be eliminated?

choices.label: A
choices.text: 'always positive, constant' and 'initially zero, steadily getting more negative'

choices.label: B
choices.text: 'always positive, steadily decreasing' and 'initially positive, decreasing, becoming negative at the end'

choices.label: C
choices.text: 'always positive, constant' and 'always positive, steadily decreasing'

choices.label: D
choices.text: 'initially zero, steadily getting more negative' and 'initially positive, decreasing, becoming negative at the end'

hints.rationale: The launch velocity is positive (not zero) and cannot stay constant under gravity, so the 'constant' and 'starts at zero' options are eliminated.

questions.stem: Which of the following is an example of a body exerting a force on itself?

choices.label: A
choices.text: a person standing up from a seated position

choices.label: B
choices.text: a car accelerating while driving

choices.label: C
choices.text: both of the above

choices.label: D
choices.text: none of the above

questions.explanation: A body cannot produce a net force on itself; acceleration always requires an external force. A person standing up pushes against the floor (which pushes back), and a car accelerates because of friction between its tires and the road. In both cases the propelling force is external.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax College Physics for AP® Courses 2e, Ch. 4 (Dynamics) — Test Prep for AP® Courses
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax; live page also posts a no-LLM-training notice — SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Stem + 4 options scraped verbatim. OpenStax withholds the public answer key; answer independently verified (acceleration requires a net external force).

hints.prompt: Can an object produce a net force on ITSELF to accelerate, with no external interaction?

choices.label: A
choices.text: no—accelerating always requires a net EXTERNAL force

choices.label: B
choices.text: yes—internal forces can accelerate the whole body

choices.label: C
choices.text: yes, but only for living things

choices.label: D
choices.text: yes, but only in a vacuum

hints.rationale: A body cannot accelerate itself; a net external force is always required.

hints.prompt: When a person stands up or a car accelerates, what supplies the external force in each case?

choices.label: A
choices.text: the floor pushing on the person, and the road (friction) pushing on the tires

choices.label: B
choices.text: the person's muscles alone, with no external contact

choices.label: C
choices.text: the car's engine acting directly on the car with no road contact

choices.label: D
choices.text: gravity alone pushing them forward

hints.rationale: Both rely on an external surface: the floor pushes the person and road friction pushes the car.

hints.prompt: Standing up uses the floor and a car uses friction from the road—both are EXTERNAL forces, not self-forces. Which pair of the individual examples can be eliminated as NOT a body forcing itself?

choices.label: A
choices.text: 'a person standing up from a seated position' and 'a car accelerating while driving'

choices.label: B
choices.text: 'both of the above' and 'none of the above'

choices.label: C
choices.text: 'a person standing up from a seated position' and 'none of the above'

choices.label: D
choices.text: 'a car accelerating while driving' and 'both of the above'

hints.rationale: Each of those uses an external surface (floor or road), so neither is a body exerting a force on itself.

questions.stem: What causes the force that moves a boat forward when someone rows it?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The force is caused by the rower's arms.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The force is caused by an interaction between the oars and gravity.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The force is caused by an interaction between the oars and the water the boat is traveling in.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The force is caused by friction.

questions.explanation: By Newton's third law, the oars push the water backward and the water pushes the oars (and the boat) forward. The forward thrust on the boat comes from this oar-water interaction.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax College Physics for AP® Courses 2e, Ch. 4 (Dynamics) — Test Prep for AP® Courses
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax; live page also posts a no-LLM-training notice — SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Stem + 4 options scraped verbatim. OpenStax withholds the public answer key; answer independently verified from Newton's third law.

hints.prompt: By Newton's third law, forward thrust on a vessel arises when it:

choices.label: A
choices.text: pushes something (like water) backward, and that something pushes it forward

choices.label: B
choices.text: pushes itself forward internally

choices.label: C
choices.text: relies only on the strength of the operator's arms

choices.label: D
choices.text: relies on gravity for forward motion

hints.rationale: Thrust is the reaction: push the medium backward and it pushes you forward.

hints.prompt: A student says the boat moves forward simply 'because of the rower's arms.' What is missing from this explanation?

choices.label: A
choices.text: the arms move the oars, but the forward thrust comes from the oars interacting with the water

choices.label: B
choices.text: nothing—the arms directly push the boat forward

choices.label: C
choices.text: the arms actually push against the air

choices.label: D
choices.text: gravity provides the forward push

hints.rationale: The arms are internal to the rower-boat system; the external forward force is the oar–water interaction.

hints.prompt: Gravity acts vertically (not forward), and the rower's arms are internal to the boat system. Which pair of options can be eliminated?

choices.label: A
choices.text: 'the force is caused by the rower's arms' and 'the force is caused by an interaction between the oars and gravity'

choices.label: B
choices.text: 'an interaction between the oars and the water' and 'the force is caused by friction'

choices.label: C
choices.text: 'the force is caused by the rower's arms' and 'an interaction between the oars and the water'

choices.label: D
choices.text: 'an interaction between the oars and gravity' and 'the force is caused by friction'

hints.rationale: Gravity is vertical and the arms are internal, so those two explanations are eliminated.

questions.stem: A ball with a mass of 0.25 kg hits a gym ceiling with a force of 78.0 N. What is the net force on the ball at that instant? (Take g = 9.8 m/s².)

choices.label: A
choices.text: 2.50 N downward

choices.label: B
choices.text: 75.5 N downward

choices.label: C
choices.text: 78.0 N downward

choices.label: D
choices.text: 80.5 N downward

questions.explanation: By Newton's third law the ceiling pushes back on the ball with 78.0 N downward. The ball's weight, mg = 0.25 × 9.8 = 2.45 N, also acts downward. The net force is 78.0 + 2.45 ≈ 80.5 N downward.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax College Physics for AP® Courses 2e, Ch. 4 (Dynamics) — Test Prep for AP® Courses
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax; live page also posts a no-LLM-training notice — SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Stem + 4 options scraped verbatim. OpenStax withholds the public answer key; answer independently verified by combining the reaction force and the weight.

hints.prompt: The net force on an object is found by:

choices.label: A
choices.text: adding all the forces acting on it as vectors

choices.label: B
choices.text: taking only the single largest force

choices.label: C
choices.text: subtracting its mass from the applied force

choices.label: D
choices.text: multiplying the forces together

hints.rationale: Net force is the vector sum of all forces on the object.

hints.prompt: At contact, two DOWNWARD forces act on the ball: the ceiling's reaction (78.0 N down, Newton's third law) and the ball's weight (mg). How do you combine them?

choices.label: A
choices.text: add them, since both point downward: 78.0 N + mg

choices.label: B
choices.text: subtract the weight from 78.0 N

choices.label: C
choices.text: use only the 78.0 N and ignore the weight

choices.label: D
choices.text: use only the weight and ignore the 78.0 N

hints.rationale: Both forces point down, so the net downward force is 78.0 N plus the weight.

hints.prompt: Because the weight ADDS to the 78.0 N (both point down), the net force must be a bit MORE than 78.0 N. Which pair of options can be eliminated as too small?

choices.label: A
choices.text: '2.50 N downward' and '75.5 N downward'

choices.label: B
choices.text: '78.0 N downward' and '80.5 N downward'

choices.label: C
choices.text: '2.50 N downward' and '80.5 N downward'

choices.label: D
choices.text: '75.5 N downward' and '78.0 N downward'

hints.rationale: Adding the weight makes the net force exceed 78.0 N, so 2.50 N and 75.5 N are far too small.

questions.stem: Which of the following is true?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Earth exerts a force due to gravity on your body, and your body exerts a smaller force on the Earth, because your mass is smaller than the mass of the Earth.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The Moon orbits the Earth because the Earth exerts a force on the Moon and the Moon exerts a force equal in magnitude and direction on the Earth.

choices.label: C
choices.text: A rocket taking off exerts a force on the Earth equal to the force the Earth exerts on the rocket.

choices.label: D
choices.text: An airplane cruising at a constant speed is not affected by gravity.

questions.explanation: Newton's third law: interacting bodies exert forces equal in magnitude and opposite in direction, regardless of their masses. So the rocket and Earth exert equal-magnitude forces on each other. Option A is wrong (the forces are equal, not smaller), B is wrong (the forces are opposite in direction), and D is wrong (gravity always acts).
questions.sourceName: OpenStax College Physics for AP® Courses 2e, Ch. 4 (Dynamics) — Test Prep for AP® Courses
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax; live page also posts a no-LLM-training notice — SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Stem + 4 options scraped verbatim. OpenStax withholds the public answer key; answer independently verified from Newton's third law.

hints.prompt: Newton's third law says two interacting bodies exert on each other forces that are:

choices.label: A
choices.text: equal in magnitude and opposite in direction, regardless of their masses

choices.label: B
choices.text: larger for the more massive body

choices.label: C
choices.text: equal in magnitude and in the same direction

choices.label: D
choices.text: zero if one body is much more massive

hints.rationale: Third-law pairs are equal in magnitude and opposite in direction, independent of mass.

hints.prompt: One statement claims Earth pulls your body but your body exerts a SMALLER force on Earth 'because your mass is smaller.' Is that consistent with Newton's third law?

choices.label: A
choices.text: no—the two forces are equal in magnitude even though the masses differ

choices.label: B
choices.text: yes—smaller mass means smaller force

choices.label: C
choices.text: yes—force is proportional to mass

choices.label: D
choices.text: no—your body actually exerts a larger force

hints.rationale: Third-law forces are equal regardless of mass, so the 'smaller force' claim is wrong.

hints.prompt: Third-law forces are equal (never smaller for the lighter object), and gravity always acts (planes are not exempt). Which pair of options can be eliminated?

choices.label: A
choices.text: 'your body exerts a smaller force on Earth because your mass is smaller' and 'an airplane cruising at constant speed is not affected by gravity'

choices.label: B
choices.text: 'the Moon and Earth exert equal-magnitude, same-direction forces' and 'a rocket exerts a force on Earth equal to the force Earth exerts on it'

choices.label: C
choices.text: 'your body exerts a smaller force on Earth...' and 'a rocket exerts a force on Earth equal to...'

choices.label: D
choices.text: 'the Moon and Earth exert equal-magnitude, same-direction forces' and 'an airplane cruising is not affected by gravity'

hints.rationale: Third-law forces are equal (not smaller) and gravity always acts, so those two statements are false and eliminated.

questions.stem: A force is applied to accelerate an object on a smooth icy surface. When the force stops, which of the following will be true? (Assume zero friction.)

choices.label: A
choices.text: The object's acceleration becomes zero.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The object's speed becomes zero.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The object's acceleration continues to increase at a constant rate.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The object accelerates, but in the opposite direction.

questions.explanation: With no applied force and no friction, the net force is zero, so by Newton's first/second law the acceleration is zero. The object keeps moving at constant velocity (its speed does not drop to zero).
questions.sourceName: OpenStax College Physics for AP® Courses 2e, Ch. 4 (Dynamics) — Test Prep for AP® Courses
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax; live page also posts a no-LLM-training notice — SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Stem + 4 options scraped verbatim. OpenStax withholds the public answer key; answer independently verified from Newton's first law.

hints.prompt: By Newton's second law, when the net force on an object is zero, its acceleration is:

choices.label: A
choices.text: zero, though it keeps moving at constant velocity

choices.label: B
choices.text: zero, and it immediately stops

choices.label: C
choices.text: constant and nonzero

choices.label: D
choices.text: steadily increasing

hints.rationale: Zero net force means zero acceleration; the object coasts at constant velocity.

hints.prompt: After the applied force stops on the frictionless ice, what is the net force on the object?

choices.label: A
choices.text: zero (no applied force, and no friction)

choices.label: B
choices.text: a backward friction force

choices.label: C
choices.text: a forward force from inertia

choices.label: D
choices.text: its weight acting horizontally

hints.rationale: With no applied force and no friction, the net horizontal force is zero.

hints.prompt: With zero net force there is nothing to speed the object up, slow it, or reverse it. Which pair of options can be eliminated?

choices.label: A
choices.text: 'the object's acceleration continues to increase at a constant rate' and 'the object accelerates, but in the opposite direction'

choices.label: B
choices.text: 'the object's acceleration becomes zero' and 'the object's speed becomes zero'

choices.label: C
choices.text: 'the object's acceleration becomes zero' and 'the object accelerates in the opposite direction'

choices.label: D
choices.text: 'the object's speed becomes zero' and 'the object's acceleration continues to increase'

hints.rationale: No net force means no increasing acceleration and no reversal, so those two are eliminated.

questions.stem: Two water-skiers, with masses of 48 kg and 61 kg, are towed behind the same boat. When the boat accelerates, the rope the skiers hold onto exerts a net force of 290 N on the skiers. At what rate will the skiers accelerate?

choices.label: B
choices.text: 2.7 m/s²

choices.label: C
choices.text: 6.0 m/s² and 4.8 m/s²

choices.label: D
choices.text: 5.3 m/s²

questions.explanation: The skiers are towed together by the single rope, so treat them as one system of mass 48 + 61 = 109 kg. a = F/m = 290 N / 109 kg ≈ 2.7 m/s². They share the same acceleration, so a single value (not two) is correct.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax College Physics for AP® Courses 2e, Ch. 4 (Dynamics) — Test Prep for AP® Courses
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax; live page also posts a no-LLM-training notice — SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Stem + 4 options scraped verbatim. OpenStax withholds the public answer key; answer independently verified by calculation (a = F/total mass).

hints.prompt: For objects towed together by a single rope, Newton's second law is best applied by:

choices.label: A
choices.text: treating them as one system with the total mass: a = F/(total mass)

choices.label: B
choices.text: using only the lighter object's mass

choices.label: C
choices.text: using only the heavier object's mass

choices.label: D
choices.text: giving each object a different acceleration

hints.rationale: Objects connected and towed together share one acceleration, a = F/(total mass).

hints.prompt: For the two skiers towed by the same rope with net force 290 N, which calculation gives their acceleration?

choices.label: B
choices.text: 290 N ÷ 48 kg and 290 N ÷ 61 kg separately

hints.rationale: The 290 N acts on the combined 109 kg system, so a = 290/109.

hints.prompt: The skiers are towed together, so they share ONE acceleration (not two), found by dividing by the combined ~109 kg. Which pair of options can be eliminated?

choices.label: A
choices.text: '6.0 m/s² and 4.8 m/s²' and '10.8 m/s²'

choices.label: B
choices.text: '2.7 m/s²' and '5.3 m/s²'

choices.label: C
choices.text: '6.0 m/s² and 4.8 m/s²' and '2.7 m/s²'

choices.label: D
choices.text: '10.8 m/s²' and '5.3 m/s²'

hints.rationale: A single shared acceleration rules out the two-value option, and dividing by 109 kg makes 10.8 m/s² far too large.

questions.stem: An archer shoots an arrow straight up with a force of 24.5 N. The arrow has a mass of 0.4 kg. What is the force of gravity on the arrow? (Take g = 9.8 m/s².)

choices.label: A
choices.text: 9.8 m/s²

questions.explanation: The force of gravity (weight) is W = mg = 0.4 kg × 9.8 m/s² = 3.92 ≈ 3.9 N. The 24.5 N launch force is a distractor, and option A has the wrong units (it is an acceleration, not a force).
questions.sourceName: OpenStax College Physics for AP® Courses 2e, Ch. 4 (Dynamics) — Test Prep for AP® Courses
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax; live page also posts a no-LLM-training notice — SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Stem + 4 options scraped verbatim. OpenStax withholds the public answer key; answer independently verified by calculation (W = mg).

hints.prompt: The force of gravity (weight) on an object is calculated as:

choices.label: A
choices.text: W = mg

choices.label: B
choices.text: W = m/g

choices.label: C
choices.text: W = m × (launch force)

choices.label: D
choices.text: W = g alone

hints.rationale: Weight is mass times gravitational acceleration, W = mg.

hints.prompt: Which numbers should you use to find the arrow's weight (g = 9.8 m/s²)?

choices.label: A
choices.text: the mass 0.4 kg times 9.8 m/s²

choices.label: B
choices.text: the 24.5 N launch force times 9.8

choices.label: C
choices.text: the mass 0.4 kg divided by 9.8

choices.label: D
choices.text: the 24.5 N launch force divided by 0.4

hints.rationale: Weight uses the mass and g (0.4 × 9.8); the launch force is irrelevant.

hints.prompt: Weight is a FORCE (unit N, not m/s²), and it should not involve the 24.5 N launch force. Which pair of options can be eliminated?
hints.rationale: '9.8 m/s²' has the wrong units for a force, and '61.25 N' misuses the launch force, so both are eliminated.

questions.stem: Two teams are engaging in a tug-of-war. The rope suddenly snaps. Which statement is true about the forces involved?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The forces exerted by the two teams are no longer equal; the teams will accelerate in opposite directions as a result.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The forces exerted by the players are no longer balanced by the force of tension in the rope; the teams will accelerate in opposite directions as a result.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The force of gravity balances the forces exerted by the players; the teams will fall as a result.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The force of tension in the rope is transferred to the players; the teams will accelerate in opposite directions as a result.

questions.explanation: While the rope is taut, each team's pull (transmitted through their feet pushing the ground) is balanced by the rope tension, so they stay in place. When the rope snaps, the tension vanishes and each team's force is no longer balanced, so each team accelerates backward—in opposite directions.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax College Physics for AP® Courses 2e, Ch. 4 (Dynamics) — Test Prep for AP® Courses
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax; live page also posts a no-LLM-training notice — SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Stem + 4 options scraped verbatim. OpenStax withholds the public answer key; answer independently verified (loss of tension unbalances each team's force).

hints.prompt: An object stays in equilibrium (no acceleration) when:

choices.label: A
choices.text: the forces on it are balanced (net force zero)

choices.label: B
choices.text: only one force acts on it

choices.label: C
choices.text: it is moving

choices.label: D
choices.text: it has a large mass

hints.rationale: Equilibrium means balanced forces / zero net force.

hints.prompt: While the rope is taut, each team's pull (through their feet on the ground) is balanced by the rope tension. What changes the instant the rope snaps?

choices.label: A
choices.text: the tension vanishes, so each team's force is no longer balanced

choices.label: B
choices.text: gravity suddenly increases

choices.label: C
choices.text: each team stops exerting any force

choices.label: D
choices.text: nothing changes

hints.rationale: Losing the tension removes the balancing force, leaving each team's push unbalanced.

hints.prompt: The two teams' pulls do not suddenly become unequal, and gravity is not the horizontal force that balanced them. Which pair of options can be eliminated?

choices.label: A
choices.text: 'the forces exerted by the two teams are no longer equal' and 'the force of gravity balances the forces exerted by the players'

choices.label: B
choices.text: 'the players' forces are no longer balanced by the tension' and 'the tension in the rope is transferred to the players'

choices.label: C
choices.text: 'the forces exerted by the two teams are no longer equal' and 'the tension is transferred to the players'

choices.label: D
choices.text: 'the force of gravity balances the players' forces' and 'the players' forces are no longer balanced by the tension'

hints.rationale: The teams' forces do not become unequal and gravity is not the horizontal balancing force, so those two are eliminated.

questions.stem: Which statement is true about drawing free-body diagrams?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Drawing a free-body diagram should be the last step in solving a problem about forces.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Drawing a free-body diagram helps you compare forces quantitatively.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The forces in a free-body diagram should always balance.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Drawing a free-body diagram can help you determine the net force.

questions.explanation: A free-body diagram shows all forces acting on a single object, which lets you add them to find the net force. It is an early step (not the last), the forces only balance in equilibrium (not always), and it primarily organizes forces rather than comparing them quantitatively.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax College Physics for AP® Courses 2e, Ch. 4 (Dynamics) — Test Prep for AP® Courses
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax; live page also posts a no-LLM-training notice — SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Stem + 4 options scraped verbatim. OpenStax withholds the public answer key; answer independently verified from the purpose of a free-body diagram.

hints.prompt: A free-body diagram is a tool that shows:

choices.label: A
choices.text: all the forces acting ON a single chosen object

choices.label: B
choices.text: the object's future path

choices.label: C
choices.text: the energy stored in the object

choices.label: D
choices.text: only the largest force

hints.rationale: A free-body diagram depicts all forces acting on one chosen object.

hints.prompt: Should a free-body diagram be the LAST step in a force problem, and do its forces ALWAYS balance?

choices.label: A
choices.text: no on both—it is an early step, and the forces balance only in equilibrium

choices.label: B
choices.text: yes—it is always the last step

choices.label: C
choices.text: yes—the forces always balance

choices.label: D
choices.text: it is the last step, but the forces need not balance

hints.rationale: A free-body diagram is drawn early, and its forces balance only when the object is in equilibrium.

hints.prompt: A free-body diagram is an EARLY step, and forces balance only in equilibrium (not always). Which pair of options can be eliminated?

choices.label: A
choices.text: 'drawing a free-body diagram should be the last step' and 'the forces in a free-body diagram should always balance'

choices.label: B
choices.text: 'it helps you compare forces quantitatively' and 'it can help you determine the net force'

choices.label: C
choices.text: 'it should be the last step' and 'it can help you determine the net force'

choices.label: D
choices.text: 'the forces should always balance' and 'it helps you compare forces quantitatively'

hints.rationale: It is an early step and its forces balance only in equilibrium, so 'last step' and 'always balance' are eliminated.

questions.stem: A block slides on a horizontal surface. The forces on it are a gravitational force of 40 N (down), a normal force of 40 N (up), and a frictional force directed to the left. The coefficient of friction between the block and the surface is 0.20. The acceleration of the block is most nearly: (Take g = 9.8 m/s².)

choices.label: A
choices.text: 1.0 m/s² to the right

choices.label: B
choices.text: 1.0 m/s² to the left

choices.label: C
choices.text: 2.0 m/s² to the right

choices.label: D
choices.text: 2.0 m/s² to the left

questions.explanation: The block's mass is m = W/g = 40/9.8 ≈ 4.08 kg. The only horizontal force is kinetic friction, f = μN = 0.20 × 40 = 8.0 N, directed left (opposing the rightward sliding). So a = f/m = 8.0/4.08 ≈ 2.0 m/s², directed to the left.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax College Physics for AP® Courses 2e, Ch. 4 (Dynamics) — Test Prep for AP® Courses
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax; live page also posts a no-LLM-training notice — SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Based on OpenStax Figure 4.48; all forces are fully specified in the question text (no image needed). OpenStax withholds the public answer key; answer independently verified by calculation.

hints.prompt: For a block on a horizontal surface where the only horizontal force is kinetic friction, its acceleration is:

choices.label: A
choices.text: a = f/m, directed the same way as the friction force

choices.label: B
choices.text: a = f × m, in the direction of motion

choices.label: C
choices.text: zero, because friction always balances out

choices.label: D
choices.text: a = mg, directed downward

hints.rationale: With friction the only horizontal force, a = f/m in friction's direction.

hints.prompt: Using W = 40 N (so m = 40/9.8 kg) and friction f = μN = 0.20 × 40 = 8.0 N, how do you get the acceleration's magnitude?

choices.label: A
choices.text: divide 8.0 N by the mass (≈ 4.08 kg)

choices.label: B
choices.text: multiply 8.0 N by 40 N

choices.label: C
choices.text: divide 40 N by 8.0 N

choices.label: D
choices.text: divide 8.0 N by 9.8

hints.prompt: The only horizontal force is friction pointing LEFT, so the acceleration must point left. Which pair of options can be eliminated?

choices.label: A
choices.text: '1.0 m/s² to the right' and '2.0 m/s² to the right'

choices.label: B
choices.text: '1.0 m/s² to the left' and '2.0 m/s² to the left'

choices.label: C
choices.text: '1.0 m/s² to the right' and '1.0 m/s² to the left'

choices.label: D
choices.text: '2.0 m/s² to the right' and '2.0 m/s² to the left'

hints.rationale: Acceleration follows the net force (friction, leftward), so both rightward options are eliminated.

questions.stem: Which phenomenon correctly describes the direction and magnitude of normal forces?

choices.label: A
choices.text: electromagnetic attraction

choices.label: B
choices.text: electromagnetic repulsion

choices.label: C
choices.text: gravitational attraction

choices.label: D
choices.text: gravitational repulsion

questions.explanation: The normal force arises from the electromagnetic repulsion between the electron clouds of atoms in the two surfaces in contact. This repulsion acts perpendicular to the surface and prevents the objects from interpenetrating.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax College Physics for AP® Courses 2e, Ch. 4 (Dynamics) — Test Prep for AP® Courses
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax; live page also posts a no-LLM-training notice — SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Stem + 4 options scraped verbatim. OpenStax withholds the public answer key; answer independently verified (normal force is electromagnetic repulsion between atoms).

hints.prompt: At the microscopic level, contact forces such as the normal force arise from:

choices.label: A
choices.text: electromagnetic interactions between the atoms of the surfaces

choices.label: B
choices.text: gravitational attraction between the surfaces

choices.label: C
choices.text: nuclear forces between the surfaces

choices.label: D
choices.text: friction alone

hints.rationale: Contact/normal forces are electromagnetic in origin, acting between surface atoms.

hints.prompt: The normal force keeps objects from passing through each other (it pushes them apart). The responsible atomic interaction must therefore be:

choices.label: A
choices.text: repulsive

choices.label: B
choices.text: attractive

choices.label: C
choices.text: zero

choices.label: D
choices.text: purely gravitational

hints.rationale: A push-apart force comes from a repulsive interaction between the surfaces' atoms.

hints.prompt: Contact forces are electromagnetic, not gravitational, in origin. Which pair of options can be eliminated?

choices.label: A
choices.text: 'gravitational attraction' and 'gravitational repulsion'

choices.label: B
choices.text: 'electromagnetic attraction' and 'electromagnetic repulsion'

choices.label: C
choices.text: 'electromagnetic attraction' and 'gravitational attraction'

choices.label: D
choices.text: 'electromagnetic repulsion' and 'gravitational repulsion'

hints.rationale: The normal force is electromagnetic, so both gravitational options are eliminated.

questions.stem: Which of the basic forces best explains tension in a rope being pulled between two people? Is the acting force causing attraction or repulsion in this instance?

choices.label: A
choices.text: gravity; attraction

choices.label: B
choices.text: electromagnetic; attraction

choices.label: C
choices.text: weak and strong nuclear; attraction

choices.label: D
choices.text: weak and strong nuclear; repulsion

questions.explanation: Tension is transmitted by the electromagnetic forces (interatomic bonds) holding the rope's molecules together. Because the rope resists being pulled apart, the relevant interatomic forces are attractive.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax College Physics for AP® Courses 2e, Ch. 4 (Dynamics) — Test Prep for AP® Courses
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax; live page also posts a no-LLM-training notice — SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Stem + 4 options scraped verbatim. OpenStax withholds the public answer key; answer independently verified (tension is electromagnetic and attractive at the atomic level).

hints.prompt: Tension in a rope is transmitted by which of the four fundamental forces?

choices.label: A
choices.text: the electromagnetic force (interatomic bonds)

choices.label: B
choices.text: gravity

choices.label: C
choices.text: the strong nuclear force

choices.label: D
choices.text: the weak nuclear force

hints.rationale: The interatomic bonds that carry tension are electromagnetic.

hints.prompt: A rope resists being pulled APART, holding its atoms together. Are the relevant interatomic forces attractive or repulsive?

choices.label: A
choices.text: attractive

choices.label: B
choices.text: repulsive

choices.label: C
choices.text: neither—they are gravitational

choices.label: D
choices.text: neither—they are nuclear

hints.rationale: Resisting being pulled apart means the interatomic bonds pull inward (attraction).

hints.prompt: Tension comes from electromagnetic interatomic bonds, not the nuclear forces. Which pair of options can be eliminated?

choices.label: A
choices.text: 'weak and strong nuclear; attraction' and 'weak and strong nuclear; repulsion'

choices.label: B
choices.text: 'gravity; attraction' and 'electromagnetic; attraction'

choices.label: C
choices.text: 'gravity; attraction' and 'weak and strong nuclear; attraction'

choices.label: D
choices.text: 'electromagnetic; attraction' and 'weak and strong nuclear; repulsion'

hints.rationale: Tension is electromagnetic, so both nuclear-force options are eliminated.

questions.stem: Jupiter has a mass approximately 300 times greater than Earth's and a radius about 11 times greater. How will the gravitational acceleration at the surface of Jupiter compare to that at the surface of the Earth?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Greater

choices.label: B
choices.text: Less

choices.label: C
choices.text: About the same

choices.label: D
choices.text: Not enough information

questions.explanation: Surface gravity is g = GM/R². Relative to Earth, Jupiter's value scales as 300/(11²) = 300/121 ≈ 2.5. Since the factor is greater than 1, Jupiter's surface gravity is greater than Earth's.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax College Physics for AP® Courses 2e, Ch. 6 (Gravitation and Uniform Circular Motion) — Test Prep for AP® Courses
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax; live page also posts a no-LLM-training notice — SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Stem + 4 options scraped verbatim. OpenStax withholds the public answer key; answer independently verified by calculation (g ∝ M/R²).

hints.prompt: Surface gravitational acceleration depends on mass and radius as:

choices.label: A
choices.text: g = GM/R² (rises with M, falls with R²)

choices.label: B
choices.text: g = GMR²

choices.label: C
choices.text: g = GR²/M

choices.label: D
choices.text: g = GM/R

hints.rationale: Surface gravity is g = GM/R².

hints.prompt: Relative to Earth, Jupiter's surface gravity scales as (mass factor) divided by (radius factor)². Which expression is that?
hints.rationale: g ∝ M/R², so the ratio is 300/11² = 300/121.

hints.prompt: The ratio 300/11² is a definite number (about 2.5), which is clearly not equal to 1. Which pair of options can be eliminated?

choices.label: A
choices.text: 'About the same' and 'Not enough information'

choices.label: B
choices.text: 'Greater' and 'Less'

choices.label: C
choices.text: 'About the same' and 'Greater'

choices.label: D
choices.text: 'Not enough information' and 'Less'

hints.rationale: The data give a definite ratio (~2.5), so 'about the same' and 'not enough information' are eliminated.

questions.stem: In the formula g = GM/r², what does G represent?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The acceleration due to gravity

choices.label: B
choices.text: A gravitational constant that is the same everywhere in the universe

choices.label: C
choices.text: A gravitational constant that is inversely proportional to the radius

choices.label: D
choices.text: The factor by which you multiply the inertial mass to obtain the gravitational mass

questions.explanation: G is the universal gravitational constant (about 6.67 × 10^-11 N·m²/kg²). It has the same value everywhere; the symbol g (lowercase) is the local acceleration due to gravity, which does depend on M and r.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax College Physics for AP® Courses 2e, Ch. 6 (Gravitation and Uniform Circular Motion) — Test Prep for AP® Courses
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax; live page also posts a no-LLM-training notice — SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Stem + 4 options scraped verbatim. OpenStax withholds the public answer key; answer independently verified (G is the universal gravitational constant).

hints.prompt: In the equation g = GM/r², the lowercase g and uppercase G are:

choices.label: A
choices.text: different—g is the local gravitational acceleration, and G is a universal constant

choices.label: B
choices.text: the same quantity written two ways

choices.label: C
choices.text: both accelerations

choices.label: D
choices.text: both dependent on location

hints.rationale: g is the local gravitational acceleration; G is the universal gravitational constant.

hints.prompt: Which quantity in g = GM/r² is the one that actually depends on the planet's mass and radius?

choices.label: A
choices.text: the lowercase g (the local acceleration)

choices.label: B
choices.text: the uppercase G

choices.label: C
choices.text: both g and G

choices.label: D
choices.text: neither of them

hints.rationale: Only g varies with M and r; G is a fixed constant, so the two must not be confused.

hints.prompt: G is a true constant (not the acceleration g, and not something that varies with radius). Which pair of options can be eliminated?

choices.label: A
choices.text: 'the acceleration due to gravity' and 'a gravitational constant that is inversely proportional to the radius'

choices.label: B
choices.text: 'a gravitational constant that is the same everywhere' and 'the factor to convert inertial mass to gravitational mass'

choices.label: C
choices.text: 'the acceleration due to gravity' and 'a gravitational constant that is the same everywhere'

choices.label: D
choices.text: 'inversely proportional to the radius' and 'the factor to convert inertial mass to gravitational mass'

hints.rationale: G is not the acceleration (that is g) and does not vary with radius, so those two are eliminated.

questions.stem: A recently discovered planet has a mass twice as great as Earth's and a radius twice as large as Earth's. What will be the approximate size of its gravitational field at the surface? (Earth's surface gravity is 9.8 m/s².)

choices.label: A
choices.text: 19 m/s²

choices.label: B
choices.text: 4.9 m/s²

choices.label: C
choices.text: 2.5 m/s²

choices.label: D
choices.text: 9.8 m/s²

questions.explanation: g = GM/R², so relative to Earth the field scales as (2)/(2²) = 2/4 = 0.5. Thus g ≈ 0.5 × 9.8 = 4.9 m/s².
questions.sourceName: OpenStax College Physics for AP® Courses 2e, Ch. 6 (Gravitation and Uniform Circular Motion) — Test Prep for AP® Courses
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax; live page also posts a no-LLM-training notice — SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Stem + 4 options scraped verbatim. OpenStax withholds the public answer key; answer independently verified by calculation (g ∝ M/R²).

hints.prompt: Surface gravity scales with mass and radius as:

choices.label: A
choices.text: g ∝ M/R²

choices.label: B
choices.text: g ∝ M·R²

choices.label: C
choices.text: g ∝ R²/M

choices.label: D
choices.text: g ∝ M/R

hints.rationale: g = GM/R², so g scales as M/R².

hints.prompt: With mass 2× and radius 2× Earth's, the surface gravity relative to Earth is which factor?
hints.rationale: g ∝ M/R² = 2/4 = 0.5, so it is half of Earth's value.

hints.prompt: Since g scales as M/R² = 2/4 = 0.5, the new gravity must be LESS than Earth's 9.8 m/s². Which pair of options can be eliminated?

choices.label: A
choices.text: '19 m/s²' and '9.8 m/s²'

choices.label: B
choices.text: '4.9 m/s²' and '2.5 m/s²'

choices.label: C
choices.text: '19 m/s²' and '4.9 m/s²'

choices.label: D
choices.text: '9.8 m/s²' and '2.5 m/s²'

hints.rationale: The factor 0.5 makes g less than 9.8 m/s², so 19 and 9.8 m/s² are eliminated.
